title: The Trinity — One God, Three Persons
intro: Before we can know who we are, we must know who God is. And to know God is to encounter a mystery that does not confuse — it overwhelms. One God. Three Persons. Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Not three gods, not one God wearing three masks, but a divine communion that has existed from before time began.
reading:
God is one. Deuteronomy 6:4 — the great Shema of Israel — declares it with unmistakable clarity: the Lord our God, the Lord is one. This is the foundation. There is no pantheon, no competing divine powers. There is one God, and He alone is Lord.

And yet this one God has revealed Himself as three distinct persons. Matthew 28:19 commissions the disciples to baptize in the name — singular — of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit. One name. Three persons. This is not a contradiction to be resolved. It is a mystery to be received.

2 Corinthians 13:14 shows us how each person relates to us distinctly: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit. The Father is the initiator — Creator, Sovereign, the one who planned salvation. The Son is the Revealer and Redeemer — God made flesh, the Word who dwelt among us. The Spirit is the Indweller and Empowerer — the one who applies what the Father planned and the Son accomplished.

Luke 3:21-22 shows all three present and active at the baptism of Jesus — the Son in the water, the Spirit descending like a dove, the Father speaking from heaven. Three persons. One God. Perfectly unified.

Ephesians 1:3-14 traces the full arc of salvation through the Trinity: the Father plans it, the Son accomplishes it, the Spirit applies and seals it. The Trinity is not a doctrine to master. It is a relationship to enter.
reflection: Which person of the Trinity do you feel most distant from right now — Father, Son, or Holy Spirit? What might that distance be revealing about where God is inviting you to draw near?
abide: Triune God — Father, Son, and Spirit — You are one, and You have drawn me into that oneness. Let me not reduce You to a doctrine. Let me know You as You are — vast, relational, and near.
